status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Smart agriculture IoT system with soil moisture sensors, weather station, irrigation automation, crop health imaging (NDVI), and yield prediction.

plan.description: Initialize the agriculture_monitoring solution scaffold: directory structure, YAML configs (project.yaml, prompts.yaml, tasks.yaml), toolchain configs for embedded (ARM GCC, CMake), Python venv for backend/ML, and Node.js for frontend. Set firmware build targets for STM32/ESP32.
acceptance_criteria: project.yaml, prompts.yaml, and tasks.yaml are valid and pass SAGE YAML validation
CMake build system compiles a hello-world firmware for ESP32-S3 without errors
`make venv` succeeds and all Python dependencies install cleanly
Frontend `npm install` succeeds with no peer-dependency errors
.env.example documents all required environment variables

plan.description: Produce a threat model (STRIDE) for the full system: sensor nodes, gateway, cloud backend, and dashboard. Identify attack surfaces (MQTT broker, OTA update channel, REST API, NDVI image upload). Define security controls per IEC 62443 SL-1/SL-2. Generate SBOM for firmware and backend dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: All 6 STRIDE threat categories covered with at least one mitigating control each
SBOM generated for firmware and backend in SPDX format
IEC 62443 security controls matrix maps each threat to a specific countermeasure
OTA update channel threat explicitly addresses firmware signing requirement
Document reviewed and signed off by safety_engineer
Device identity provisioned
Firmware signing enabled

plan.description: Establish IEC 62443 compliance evidence package: zone/conduit model, security lifecycle plan, patch management policy, and incident response procedure. Create a traceability matrix linking security requirements to implementation tasks.
acceptance_criteria: Zone/conduit diagram covers all four network zones with defined trust boundaries
Traceability matrix links every IEC 62443 clause to at least one implementation task or evidence artifact
Patch management policy specifies maximum days-to-patch per severity level
Incident response procedure defines roles and escalation paths
Artifacts stored in compliance/ directory and referenced in project.yaml

plan.description: Design the soil moisture + environmental sensor node PCB: ESP32-S3 MCU, capacitive soil moisture sensor interface (ADC), DHT22 temperature/humidity, 4G/LoRa radio module, LiPo battery management (TP4056), and solar charging input. Generate schematic, BOM, and DRC-clean layout.
acceptance_criteria: KiCad DRC passes with zero errors and zero unconnected nets
BOM includes part numbers, quantities, and supplier links for all components
Gerber files pass online DFM check (JLCPCB or equivalent)
Power budget analysis shows >72h battery life at 15-min sampling interval
Schematic reviewed and annotated with test points for all critical signals

plan.description: Implement ESP32-S3 firmware for the soil moisture sensor node: FreeRTOS tasks for ADC sampling (soil moisture, temperature, humidity, light), deep-sleep power management (15-min wake cycle), LoRa/MQTT data transmission with TLS 1.2, signed OTA update support, and sensor calibration routines.
acceptance_criteria: Firmware compiles cleanly for ESP32-S3 target with zero warnings at -Wall
Deep sleep current draw < 50µA measured via INA219
MQTT publish succeeds with TLS 1.2 mutual authentication to broker
OTA update rejects unsigned firmware with explicit error log
Soil moisture reading reproducibility < 2% across 10 consecutive samples
All FreeRTOS tasks have defined stack sizes and priority levels documented
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Implement STM32L4-based weather station firmware: HAL drivers for anemometer (pulse counter), rain gauge (tipping bucket interrupt), barometric pressure (BMP390 I2C), UV index (VEML6075 I2C), and ambient temperature. Aggregate readings into weather packets and transmit via LoRa to the gateway.
acceptance_criteria: Firmware compiles for STM32L4 with STM32CubeIDE toolchain, zero errors
Wind speed measurement validated against reference anemometer ± 0.5 m/s
Rain gauge debounce correctly counts tipping bucket pulses under 100ms bursts
BMP390 pressure readings within ±1 hPa of calibration reference
LoRa packet transmission confirmed received by gateway test harness
Power consumption in sleep mode < 10µA between 10-minute sample intervals
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Implement irrigation controller firmware on ESP32-S3: control relay bank (8-zone solenoid valves) via GPIO, receive irrigation schedules via MQTT, execute timed and threshold-triggered watering cycles, log flow meter pulses for water volume accounting, and enforce safety interlocks (max run time, dry-run protection).
acceptance_criteria: Relay actuation latency < 100ms from MQTT command receipt
Max runtime interlock triggers and closes all valves after configured threshold
Flow meter pulse counting accurate to ±5% vs. reference graduated cylinder at 2 L/min
Dry-run protection disables zone if flow < 0.1 L/min after 30s of valve-open
Schedule engine correctly handles timezone offset and DST transitions
All safety interlocks log events to MQTT topic `agriculture/irrigation/events`
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Implement NDVI imaging module firmware on Raspberry Pi CM4: drive OV5647 visible + modified NIR camera pair, trigger synchronized dual captures, compute per-pixel NDVI = (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red), generate false-color PNG overlays, and publish image metadata + NDVI statistics to MQTT. Implement secure image upload to backend S3 bucket.
acceptance_criteria: Dual-camera capture synchronized within 50ms
NDVI computation produces values in [-1.0, 1.0] range for all pixels
False-color PNG heatmap renders in < 5s on CM4 for 5MP image
NDVI statistics (mean, std, min, max, healthy_pixel_pct) published to MQTT within 10s of capture
S3 upload uses presigned URLs with 15-minute expiry and TLS 1.2
System validates image sharpness (Laplacian variance > threshold) and rejects blurry captures
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Design and implement the time-series and relational database schema: PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB for sensor telemetry (soil moisture, weather, irrigation events), PostGIS extension for field zone geospatial data, and object metadata table for NDVI images. Include migration scripts and seed data for development.

payload.seed_data: 3 field zones, 5 sensor nodes, 30 days historical data

acceptance_criteria: All Alembic migrations apply cleanly on a fresh PostgreSQL 16 instance
TimescaleDB hypertables created with 1-day chunk intervals for all telemetry tables
PostGIS geometry columns accept GeoJSON polygon input for field zones
Indexes on (node_id, timestamp) yield query times < 50ms for 30-day range queries
Seed data script populates all tables and is idempotent on re-run
Schema ER diagram generated and stored in docs/database_schema.png

plan.description: Build the FastAPI backend service: MQTT subscriber ingesting sensor telemetry into TimescaleDB, REST endpoints for sensor data retrieval, field zone management, irrigation schedule CRUD, NDVI image metadata, and yield predictions. Implement JWT authentication, rate limiting, and structured logging.
acceptance_criteria: MQTT subscriber processes 100 sensor messages/sec without dropping events
All REST endpoints return correct HTTP status codes per OpenAPI spec
JWT validation rejects expired and tampered tokens with 401 responses
Rate limiter enforces 100 req/min per authenticated user
Structured logs include trace_id, user_id, and request duration for every request
`make test-api` passes all endpoint integration tests against a live test database

plan.description: Produce the complete OpenAPI 3.1 specification for all agriculture_monitoring REST endpoints: sensor telemetry, field zones, irrigation scheduling, NDVI image metadata, yield predictions, and auth. Include request/response schemas, error codes, and example payloads.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates cleanly with `openapi-generator validate`
Every endpoint has at least one request example and one response example
All error responses (400, 401, 403, 404, 422, 500) documented with schema
Spec is auto-generated from FastAPI route decorators and kept in sync with implementation
Static ReDoc HTML renders all endpoints browsable without a live server

plan.description: Build the sensor data pipeline: raw MQTT telemetry → validation → normalization → TimescaleDB ingest → 15-min and 1-hour downsampled continuous aggregates. Implement anomaly detection for out-of-range sensor readings (soil moisture > 100%, temperature spikes) and dead-node alerting.
acceptance_criteria: Pipeline processes 1000 simulated sensor events with zero data loss under load test
Out-of-range readings are flagged with `anomaly: true` and logged to alerts table
15-min and 1-hour continuous aggregates materialize within 5s of data insertion
Dead-node alert fires within 35 minutes of last heartbeat (5 min tolerance)
Validation rejects malformed JSON payloads with structured error log entries
Data pipeline throughput benchmarked and result stored in docs/performance_benchmarks.md

plan.description: Train and deploy an NDVI-based crop health classification model: binary healthy/stressed classifier using NDVI statistics + weather context features, trained on publicly available crop NDVI datasets. Export as ONNX, deploy as a FastAPI inference microservice, and integrate with the main backend.

payload.training_data: Sentinel-2 NDVI crop health public dataset

acceptance_criteria: Model achieves >= 85% accuracy and >= 0.80 F1 score on held-out test set
ONNX export validates with onnxruntime and produces identical predictions to training framework
Inference endpoint responds in < 200ms for single prediction requests
Model card (model_card.md) documents training data, metrics, and known limitations
Unit tests cover feature preprocessing pipeline with known input/output pairs
Model version tracked in MLflow or equivalent experiment tracker

plan.description: Build and deploy a yield prediction model: regression model predicting crop yield (kg/hectare) from historical soil moisture, weather, NDVI time series, and irrigation history. Use an LSTM or temporal fusion transformer for time-series inputs. Deploy as `/ml/yield-prediction` endpoint.

payload.training_data: USDA NASS historical crop yield + synthetic data augmentation

acceptance_criteria: Model achieves MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) <= 15% on held-out test fields
Prediction includes 80% confidence interval bounds
Model retraining pipeline is scriptable with `python ml/yield_prediction/train.py --data <path>`
Inference endpoint handles batch requests of up to 50 fields in < 2s
Feature importance report generated and stored in ml/yield_prediction/feature_importance.png
Prediction results stored in `yield_predictions` DB table with model version tag

plan.description: Build the React 18 + TypeScript dashboard: field map view (Leaflet.js with GeoJSON zones), real-time sensor telemetry charts (Recharts), NDVI heatmap viewer, irrigation schedule calendar, yield prediction panel, and alert inbox. Implement JWT-based auth flow and WebSocket for live sensor updates.
acceptance_criteria: Field map renders GeoJSON field zones with correct coordinates on Leaflet
Sensor charts update in real-time via WebSocket with < 2s latency
NDVI heatmap displays uploaded PNG overlaid on field map with opacity control
Irrigation scheduler allows creating, editing, and deleting schedules with date/time picker
Yield prediction panel renders point estimate and confidence interval as chart
Lighthouse performance score >= 80 on desktop; all pages load < 3s on 4G throttle
JWT tokens stored in httpOnly cookies, not localStorage

plan.description: Produce UX wireframes and interaction design for the farmer-facing dashboard: field map interaction model, sensor alert workflow, irrigation manual override flow, and NDVI interpretation guide. Conduct accessibility audit (WCAG 2.1 AA) on implemented frontend.
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes cover all 7 frontend pages with annotated interaction states
Irrigation manual override flow is completable in <= 3 clicks from the field map
NDVI interpretation guide uses color-blind-safe palette validated with Colour Contrast Analyser
axe-core audit reports zero critical and zero serious accessibility violations on all pages
Wireframes reviewed and approved by at least one representative farmer persona

plan.description: Write HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop) test specifications and firmware unit tests: soil moisture ADC calibration test, LoRa packet integrity test under RF attenuation, irrigation relay timing accuracy test, OTA update success/rollback test, and NDVI capture synchronization test. Implement firmware unit tests with Unity framework.

payload.hil_environment: ESP32 devkit + bench instruments (oscilloscope, DMM, signal generator)

acceptance_criteria: Unity test suite achieves >= 90% branch coverage of firmware business logic
HIL test plan documents setup, procedure, pass/fail criteria for each test case
OTA rollback test confirms device boots previous firmware after simulated failed update
Relay timing test confirms valve open/close latency < 150ms across all 8 zones
LoRa RF margin test passes at -10dB simulated path loss attenuation
All HIL tests have documented expected vs. actual results format

plan.description: Write backend unit and integration tests: pytest suite for all API endpoints (sensor ingest, irrigation CRUD, NDVI metadata, yield predictions), MQTT subscriber integration test with Mosquitto test broker, data pipeline anomaly detection tests, and ML model inference contract tests.
acceptance_criteria: pytest suite achieves >= 85% line coverage across backend src/
MQTT integration test sends 500 messages and verifies all stored in TimescaleDB
All API endpoint tests assert correct status codes, response schemas, and error cases
ML inference contract tests assert prediction output schema stability across model versions
Tests run in < 3 minutes in CI with parallel workers
`make test` exits with code 0 on clean main branch

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system integration tests: simulated sensor node → MQTT broker → backend ingest → DB → API → frontend data flow, full irrigation cycle (schedule trigger → valve open → flow measurement → valve close → event log), and NDVI capture → ML inference → dashboard display pipeline.

payload.test_environment: Docker Compose (all services) + simulated sensor scripts

acceptance_criteria: All 5 E2E scenarios pass 5 consecutive runs without flakiness
Sensor data appears on frontend dashboard within 5s of simulated transmission
Irrigation cycle completes end-to-end and event appears in audit log within 10s
NDVI analysis pipeline produces dashboard heatmap within 60s of simulated capture
Load test: 50 concurrent sensor nodes at 1 msg/min sustains < 5% error rate over 10 minutes
System test report generated and stored as CI artifact

plan.description: Set up CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions): firmware build + Unity test stage, backend pytest + coverage gate, frontend lint + Playwright E2E stage, Docker image build and push to GHCR, and deployment to staging environment. Add Prometheus + Grafana monitoring and Alertmanager for production.
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline completes in < 15 minutes for a standard PR
Coverage gate fails the pipeline if backend coverage drops below 85%
Docker images tagged with git SHA and pushed to GHCR on every main branch merge
Prometheus scrapes backend `/metrics` endpoint and Grafana dashboard displays sensor ingest rate
Alertmanager fires Slack notification when MQTT ingest error rate exceeds 1% for 5 minutes
Staging deployment is fully automated on merge to main with zero manual steps

plan.description: Provision production infrastructure via Terraform: VPC with public/private subnets, ECS Fargate for backend and ML inference services, RDS PostgreSQL 16 with TimescaleDB extension, S3 bucket for NDVI images, IoT Core MQTT broker (or self-hosted Mosquitto on EC2), and CloudFront CDN for frontend.
acceptance_criteria: `terraform plan` produces zero errors on a clean AWS account with correct variables
`terraform apply` provisions all resources in < 20 minutes
RDS instance has TimescaleDB extension enabled and accessible from ECS tasks only (no public endpoint)
S3 bucket has versioning enabled, public access blocked, and bucket policy requiring TLS
All resources tagged with Environment, Project, and ManagedBy tags
Terraform state stored in S3 backend with DynamoDB state lock

plan.description: Conduct FMEA and hazard analysis for the irrigation automation system: identify failure modes (valve stuck open causing waterlogging, sensor failure causing over/under-irrigation, controller power loss during active cycle). Assign severity/probability ratings and define mitigating safeguards.
acceptance_criteria: FMEA covers all identified failure modes with Severity, Occurrence, and Detection ratings
Any RPN > 100 has a defined corrective action and responsibility owner
Hazard analysis identifies at least 5 hazardous events with severity classification
Safety requirements derived from FMEA are traceable to firmware safety interlocks
Document reviewed and signed by safety_engineer
Safety requirements referenced in traceability matrix from step 3

plan.description: Produce complete project documentation: system architecture diagram, firmware developer guide (build + flash + debug workflow), API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI), field deployment guide (sensor node installation + calibration), and operator manual for the dashboard.
acceptance_criteria: Architecture diagram shows all system components and data flows end-to-end
Firmware dev guide enables a new developer to build and flash firmware from scratch following written steps only
Field deployment guide includes illustrated sensor node mounting instructions and calibration procedure
Operator manual covers all 7 dashboard pages with annotated screenshots
All documentation spell-checked and cross-linked (no broken internal links)
README includes quick-start instructions that work on a fresh Ubuntu 22.04 machine

flaws: FMEA/hazard analysis (step 22) runs AFTER all firmware is implemented (steps 5-8). Safety analysis must precede design to influence interlocks — doing it after is documentation theater, not safety engineering. This inverts the purpose of FMEA.

UX design and accessibility audit (step 16) happens AFTER the frontend is fully built (step 15). Wireframes exist to guide implementation, not to review it post-hoc. You will discover interaction model problems after the React code is written.

PCB design (step 4) depends only on step 1, ignoring the threat model (step 2) entirely. Hardware security provisions — secure element, hardware RNG, TPM, RF shielding decisions — should be informed by the STRIDE analysis. A PCB that ignores the threat model must be respun.

No LoRa gateway step anywhere in the plan. The sensor nodes (ESP32-S3, STM32L4) transmit LoRaWAN; the backend receives MQTT. The LoRa-to-MQTT gateway is a distinct hardware+software component requiring its own firmware, backhaul configuration, and network planning. It is simply absent.

TimescaleDB on AWS RDS PostgreSQL 16 (step 21) is unsupported. AWS RDS does not allow loading arbitrary extensions like TimescaleDB. You must use RDS Aurora PostgreSQL without TimescaleDB, a self-managed EC2 PostgreSQL instance, or Timescale Cloud. The Terraform plan will fail at extension installation.

ML model training uses Sentinel-2 satellite imagery (10m resolution, top-of-atmosphere reflectance) as training data for a model that will run inference on ground-level OV5647 camera NDVI values. The domain gap between satellite and in-field handheld/mounted imagery is enormous. This model will produce garbage predictions in production.

STM32L4 OTA over LoRaWAN (step 6 acceptance criteria) is listed as a simple checkbox but LoRaWAN FUOTA requires the fragmentation and reassembly protocol (TS004), takes hours per update due to duty cycle limits, and requires network server support. Treating it as routine is a critical underestimate.

Dual-camera synchronized capture on RPi CM4 (step 8) assumes hardware sync within 50ms. The OV5647 does not have a hardware VSYNC trigger output. Achieving sub-50ms synchronization using software CSI-2 triggers under a loaded Linux kernel is unreliable. True synchronization requires an external frame-sync GPIO pulse and modified camera drivers.

Step 5 firmware spec lists both LoRaWAN 1.0.4 AND MQTT over TLS simultaneously on the ESP32-S3. LoRaWAN is a constrained radio protocol — it does not carry IP traffic. MQTT requires IP. These are two separate connectivity paths requiring a gateway bridge. The architecture conflates them without defining when each is used.

Device provisioning infrastructure is entirely missing. Step 2 states 'Device identity provisioned' as an acceptance criterion, but no step builds the provisioning pipeline: certificate authority, device certificate issuance, secure storage in manufacturing, and field commissioning workflow. Field-deploying hundreds of nodes without this is operationally impossible.

Power budget (step 4 acceptance criteria) requires 72h battery life but the PCB includes both SX1276 (LoRa) and SIM7600 (4G). SIM7600 peaks at 2A during transmission. Running both radios with a 3.7V LiPo at 15-min intervals will likely halve the calculated battery life. The power budget analysis must explicitly model radio duty cycles for both paths.

USDA NASS yield data used for TFT training (step 14) is US county-level aggregated data. Applying it to individual field-level prediction globally is a dataset mismatch. The model will have poor generalization outside US commodity crops and the 'synthetic data augmentation' hand-wave does not resolve this.

WebSocket authentication (step 15) combined with httpOnly JWT cookies is not addressed in the backend (step 10). WebSocket upgrade requests from browsers do not automatically include httpOnly cookies in all configurations, and the backend step only specifies JWT RS256 token return — not cookie-based session issuance. This creates an auth gap on the real-time feed.
suggestions: Reorder: threat model (step 2) and FMEA (step 22) must both precede PCB design (step 4) and firmware (steps 5-8). Collapse them into steps 2-3 before any hardware design work begins.

Add a dedicated LoRa Gateway step between firmware (steps 5-6) and backend (step 10): gateway hardware selection, Chirpstack or TTN server configuration, MQTT bridge setup, and backhaul connectivity planning.
Move UX design to immediately after the API spec (step 11) and before frontend implementation (step 15). Wireframes are inputs, not outputs.

Replace RDS TimescaleDB assumption with a concrete decision: either Timescale Cloud (managed, works with Terraform), self-hosted PostgreSQL on EC2 with TimescaleDB, or drop TimescaleDB in favor of native PostgreSQL partitioning + pg_cron for aggregates.

For the crop health ML model (step 13), collect a ground-truth calibration dataset from the actual deployed cameras during system integration testing. The Sentinel-2 data can seed initial training, but the model must be fine-tuned on in-field captures before any accuracy claim is meaningful.

Add a device provisioning step: define the CA hierarchy, provisioning server API, and manufacturing-time certificate injection workflow. AWS IoT Core Just-In-Time-Provisioning or a custom provisioning lambda are valid approaches — but it needs an explicit step.

Define the LoRaWAN FUOTA strategy explicitly in step 6: which network server supports it, what the maximum firmware size is given LoRa duty cycle, and what the SLA for an OTA update is (hours, not minutes).

For dual-camera sync (step 8), redesign to use hardware frame-sync: one camera as master drives a GPIO pulse to the second camera's FSIN pin. Document this in the PCB schematic as a required trace and update step 4 acceptance criteria accordingly.

Add a secrets management step in the DevOps/Infra section: AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault for JWT private keys, DB credentials, MQTT client certificates. Currently secrets handling is implicit throughout and will result in credentials in environment variables or Docker compose files.

Add a data governance and retention policy step: field location, yield, and crop data is commercially sensitive. Define retention periods, encryption at rest requirements, and data residency constraints before building the schema.
missing: LoRa gateway hardware and software step — this is a load-bearing system component with no implementation step
Device identity provisioning infrastructure — no CA, no certificate issuance pipeline, no manufacturing workflow
Field network topology and backhaul planning — 4G dead zones, LoRa gateway placement, fallback connectivity
Data governance, retention, and privacy impact assessment
Production secrets management (Vault, Secrets Manager, or equivalent)
Camera hardware sync circuit specification in PCB design
LoRaWAN network server selection and configuration (Chirpstack, TTN, AWS IoT Core LoRaWAN)
Irrigation actuator hardware safety validation — physical pressure test, valve specification review, water hammer analysis
Multi-farm/multi-tenant data isolation strategy — one backend serving multiple farms with strict data segregation
Rollback and disaster recovery procedures for the production infrastructure
security_risks: No firmware signing key management plan. Step 5 requires RSA2048 firmware signing, but the private key storage, rotation policy, and revocation procedure are never defined. A compromised signing key means every deployed device is vulnerable.

MQTT broker (Mosquitto with TLS) is listed but mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate provisioning is not addressed. If sensors authenticate with username/password instead of client certs, an attacker who captures credentials can inject arbitrary telemetry, triggering false irrigation cycles.

Irrigation automation with no out-of-band kill switch. If the MQTT broker or backend is compromised, an attacker can open all 8 solenoid valves simultaneously. The firmware safety interlocks (step 7) are the only safeguard — there is no hardware emergency stop or physical override requirement defined.

NDVI image upload via presigned S3 URLs (step 8) — if the presigned URL generation endpoint lacks rate limiting or proper authentication scope, an attacker could generate unlimited upload URLs and exhaust S3 storage or inject malicious images into the ML pipeline.

LoRa RF link has no mention of LoRaWAN join server security or AppKey/NwkKey rotation. Default ABP activation with static keys is common in agricultural LoRa deployments and makes replay attacks and message forgery trivial.

No mention of firmware secure boot on ESP32-S3 (eFuse burning, flash encryption). Without secure boot enabled in hardware, OTA signing is bypassed by anyone with physical flash access — trivial in a field-deployed device.

JWT RS256 private key storage on ECS Fargate containers is unspecified. If stored as an environment variable in the task definition, it appears in CloudTrail logs and ECS task metadata endpoint — accessible to any process in the container.
final_review.summary: This plan is architecturally ambitious and covers the right domains — hardware, firmware, backend, ML, frontend, and compliance — but has critical sequencing inversions and a missing load-bearing component that require fundamental rework before execution. The FMEA after firmware, UX after frontend, and PCB before threat model inversions are not minor oversights: they mean safety requirements cannot inform implementation, accessibility problems will be discovered in retrofitting, and hardware security provisions will be missing from the physical design. The absent LoRa gateway step is a blocking gap — the system physically cannot function without it and no step builds it. The TimescaleDB-on-RDS assumption will cause the Terraform apply to fail. The ML crop health model trained on Sentinel-2 satellite data will not generalize to ground-mounted cameras and should not be evaluated against an accuracy target without domain adaptation. The firmware OTA over LoRaWAN acceptance criterion is stated as a checkbox but represents weeks of engineering given LoRa duty-cycle constraints. Security is thoughtful at the design level (IEC 62443, STRIDE) but execution-level gaps — signing key management, secure boot enablement, MQTT client certificate provisioning pipeline — would leave the deployed system vulnerable despite the compliance documentation. Reordering steps 2/3/22 before step 4, adding a gateway step, resolving the RDS constraint, and grounding the ML training data strategy would bring this to a shippable plan.

flaws: FMEA/hazard analysis (step 22) runs AFTER all firmware is implemented (steps 5-8). Safety analysis must precede design to influence interlocks — doing it after is documentation theater, not safety engineering. This inverts the purpose of FMEA.

UX design and accessibility audit (step 16) happens AFTER the frontend is fully built (step 15). Wireframes exist to guide implementation, not to review it post-hoc. You will discover interaction model problems after the React code is written.

PCB design (step 4) depends only on step 1, ignoring the threat model (step 2) entirely. Hardware security provisions — secure element, hardware RNG, TPM, RF shielding decisions — should be informed by the STRIDE analysis. A PCB that ignores the threat model must be respun.

No LoRa gateway step anywhere in the plan. The sensor nodes (ESP32-S3, STM32L4) transmit LoRaWAN; the backend receives MQTT. The LoRa-to-MQTT gateway is a distinct hardware+software component requiring its own firmware, backhaul configuration, and network planning. It is simply absent.

TimescaleDB on AWS RDS PostgreSQL 16 (step 21) is unsupported. AWS RDS does not allow loading arbitrary extensions like TimescaleDB. You must use RDS Aurora PostgreSQL without TimescaleDB, a self-managed EC2 PostgreSQL instance, or Timescale Cloud. The Terraform plan will fail at extension installation.

ML model training uses Sentinel-2 satellite imagery (10m resolution, top-of-atmosphere reflectance) as training data for a model that will run inference on ground-level OV5647 camera NDVI values. The domain gap between satellite and in-field handheld/mounted imagery is enormous. This model will produce garbage predictions in production.

STM32L4 OTA over LoRaWAN (step 6 acceptance criteria) is listed as a simple checkbox but LoRaWAN FUOTA requires the fragmentation and reassembly protocol (TS004), takes hours per update due to duty cycle limits, and requires network server support. Treating it as routine is a critical underestimate.

Dual-camera synchronized capture on RPi CM4 (step 8) assumes hardware sync within 50ms. The OV5647 does not have a hardware VSYNC trigger output. Achieving sub-50ms synchronization using software CSI-2 triggers under a loaded Linux kernel is unreliable. True synchronization requires an external frame-sync GPIO pulse and modified camera drivers.

Step 5 firmware spec lists both LoRaWAN 1.0.4 AND MQTT over TLS simultaneously on the ESP32-S3. LoRaWAN is a constrained radio protocol — it does not carry IP traffic. MQTT requires IP. These are two separate connectivity paths requiring a gateway bridge. The architecture conflates them without defining when each is used.

Device provisioning infrastructure is entirely missing. Step 2 states 'Device identity provisioned' as an acceptance criterion, but no step builds the provisioning pipeline: certificate authority, device certificate issuance, secure storage in manufacturing, and field commissioning workflow. Field-deploying hundreds of nodes without this is operationally impossible.

Power budget (step 4 acceptance criteria) requires 72h battery life but the PCB includes both SX1276 (LoRa) and SIM7600 (4G). SIM7600 peaks at 2A during transmission. Running both radios with a 3.7V LiPo at 15-min intervals will likely halve the calculated battery life. The power budget analysis must explicitly model radio duty cycles for both paths.

USDA NASS yield data used for TFT training (step 14) is US county-level aggregated data. Applying it to individual field-level prediction globally is a dataset mismatch. The model will have poor generalization outside US commodity crops and the 'synthetic data augmentation' hand-wave does not resolve this.

WebSocket authentication (step 15) combined with httpOnly JWT cookies is not addressed in the backend (step 10). WebSocket upgrade requests from browsers do not automatically include httpOnly cookies in all configurations, and the backend step only specifies JWT RS256 token return — not cookie-based session issuance. This creates an auth gap on the real-time feed.
suggestions: Reorder: threat model (step 2) and FMEA (step 22) must both precede PCB design (step 4) and firmware (steps 5-8). Collapse them into steps 2-3 before any hardware design work begins.

Add a dedicated LoRa Gateway step between firmware (steps 5-6) and backend (step 10): gateway hardware selection, Chirpstack or TTN server configuration, MQTT bridge setup, and backhaul connectivity planning.
Move UX design to immediately after the API spec (step 11) and before frontend implementation (step 15). Wireframes are inputs, not outputs.

Replace RDS TimescaleDB assumption with a concrete decision: either Timescale Cloud (managed, works with Terraform), self-hosted PostgreSQL on EC2 with TimescaleDB, or drop TimescaleDB in favor of native PostgreSQL partitioning + pg_cron for aggregates.

For the crop health ML model (step 13), collect a ground-truth calibration dataset from the actual deployed cameras during system integration testing. The Sentinel-2 data can seed initial training, but the model must be fine-tuned on in-field captures before any accuracy claim is meaningful.

Add a device provisioning step: define the CA hierarchy, provisioning server API, and manufacturing-time certificate injection workflow. AWS IoT Core Just-In-Time-Provisioning or a custom provisioning lambda are valid approaches — but it needs an explicit step.

Define the LoRaWAN FUOTA strategy explicitly in step 6: which network server supports it, what the maximum firmware size is given LoRa duty cycle, and what the SLA for an OTA update is (hours, not minutes).

For dual-camera sync (step 8), redesign to use hardware frame-sync: one camera as master drives a GPIO pulse to the second camera's FSIN pin. Document this in the PCB schematic as a required trace and update step 4 acceptance criteria accordingly.

Add a secrets management step in the DevOps/Infra section: AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault for JWT private keys, DB credentials, MQTT client certificates. Currently secrets handling is implicit throughout and will result in credentials in environment variables or Docker compose files.

Add a data governance and retention policy step: field location, yield, and crop data is commercially sensitive. Define retention periods, encryption at rest requirements, and data residency constraints before building the schema.
missing: LoRa gateway hardware and software step — this is a load-bearing system component with no implementation step
Device identity provisioning infrastructure — no CA, no certificate issuance pipeline, no manufacturing workflow
Field network topology and backhaul planning — 4G dead zones, LoRa gateway placement, fallback connectivity
Data governance, retention, and privacy impact assessment
Production secrets management (Vault, Secrets Manager, or equivalent)
Camera hardware sync circuit specification in PCB design
LoRaWAN network server selection and configuration (Chirpstack, TTN, AWS IoT Core LoRaWAN)
Irrigation actuator hardware safety validation — physical pressure test, valve specification review, water hammer analysis
Multi-farm/multi-tenant data isolation strategy — one backend serving multiple farms with strict data segregation
Rollback and disaster recovery procedures for the production infrastructure
security_risks: No firmware signing key management plan. Step 5 requires RSA2048 firmware signing, but the private key storage, rotation policy, and revocation procedure are never defined. A compromised signing key means every deployed device is vulnerable.

MQTT broker (Mosquitto with TLS) is listed but mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate provisioning is not addressed. If sensors authenticate with username/password instead of client certs, an attacker who captures credentials can inject arbitrary telemetry, triggering false irrigation cycles.

Irrigation automation with no out-of-band kill switch. If the MQTT broker or backend is compromised, an attacker can open all 8 solenoid valves simultaneously. The firmware safety interlocks (step 7) are the only safeguard — there is no hardware emergency stop or physical override requirement defined.

NDVI image upload via presigned S3 URLs (step 8) — if the presigned URL generation endpoint lacks rate limiting or proper authentication scope, an attacker could generate unlimited upload URLs and exhaust S3 storage or inject malicious images into the ML pipeline.

LoRa RF link has no mention of LoRaWAN join server security or AppKey/NwkKey rotation. Default ABP activation with static keys is common in agricultural LoRa deployments and makes replay attacks and message forgery trivial.

No mention of firmware secure boot on ESP32-S3 (eFuse burning, flash encryption). Without secure boot enabled in hardware, OTA signing is bypassed by anyone with physical flash access — trivial in a field-deployed device.

JWT RS256 private key storage on ECS Fargate containers is unspecified. If stored as an environment variable in the task definition, it appears in CloudTrail logs and ECS task metadata endpoint — accessible to any process in the container.
history.summary: This plan is architecturally ambitious and covers the right domains — hardware, firmware, backend, ML, frontend, and compliance — but has critical sequencing inversions and a missing load-bearing component that require fundamental rework before execution. The FMEA after firmware, UX after frontend, and PCB before threat model inversions are not minor oversights: they mean safety requirements cannot inform implementation, accessibility problems will be discovered in retrofitting, and hardware security provisions will be missing from the physical design. The absent LoRa gateway step is a blocking gap — the system physically cannot function without it and no step builds it. The TimescaleDB-on-RDS assumption will cause the Terraform apply to fail. The ML crop health model trained on Sentinel-2 satellite data will not generalize to ground-mounted cameras and should not be evaluated against an accuracy target without domain adaptation. The firmware OTA over LoRaWAN acceptance criterion is stated as a checkbox but represents weeks of engineering given LoRa duty-cycle constraints. Security is thoughtful at the design level (IEC 62443, STRIDE) but execution-level gaps — signing key management, secure boot enablement, MQTT client certificate provisioning pipeline — would leave the deployed system vulnerable despite the compliance documentation. Reordering steps 2/3/22 before step 4, adding a gateway step, resolving the RDS constraint, and grounding the ML training data strategy would bring this to a shippable plan.
